register_intro: This is your building's private forum. Report maintenance issues, document landlord disputes, share community news, and keep your neighbors informed. Everything here is timestamped and photographed — building a record that matters.
register_home_info: This is your home on TENANTNET.NYC.
register_tip_1: Post issues with photos for evidence
register_tip_3: Your posts are timestamped and documented
register_error_password_length: Password must be at least 8 characters
register_error_unknown: Something went wrong. Please try again.
report_desc: Send a report to your landlord, management company, HPD, 311, or any email address. Replies will go to your building's tenant rep.
report_message_placeholder: Describe the issue in detail. Include dates, unit number, and any prior communication...
report_how_desc: This email will be sent from your building's TENANTNET.NYC address. You will be CC'd so you stay on the thread with the recipient. Replies go to you and the tenant representative.
report_sent_desc: You were CC'd — check your inbox for the thread. Replies go to you and the tenant rep.
auth_invalid_desc: This QR code is no longer valid. It may have been rotated by your tenant manager.
auth_invalid_scan: Please scan the new QR code on your door.
upload_invalid_type: Invalid file type. Accepted: jpeg, png, webp
records_load: Load Records from NYC Open Data
records_none: No records found — that's a good sign.
records_stab_none: No registration data found for this building.
records_stab_check_desc: NYC tracks stabilization at the building level. To verify your specific apartment's status, request your rent history from DHCR at
records_stab_view_hpd: View full HPD registration on NYC website
records_stab_desc: View rent stabilization unit counts from NYC public records.
records_desc_hpd_violations: Housing code violations from the NYC Department of Housing Preservation & Development.
records_desc_hpd_complaints: Housing complaints filed with HPD for issues like heat, hot water, pests, and more.
records_desc_dob_violations: Building code violations from the NYC Department of Buildings.
records_desc_dob_complaints: Complaints filed with the Department of Buildings about construction, safety, and permits.
records_desc_default: NYC public records for this building.
rs_title: Your building is on record as rent stabilized
rs_source: Source: NYC HPD Building Registrations (Open Data)
rs_intro: According to NYC records, this building is rent stabilized. This means your rent increases are regulated by law, and you have important protections including lease renewal rights and limits on how much your landlord can raise your rent.
rs_unit_level: Stabilization status is tracked at the building level in NYC databases. To confirm your individual apartment's status, you need to request your rent history from DHCR (see step 1 below).
rs_check_title: How to check if your apartment has been illegally deregulated
rs_step_1: Request your apartment's rent history from the NYS Division of Housing and Community Renewal (DHCR). This is the only way to verify unit-level stabilization.
rs_step_1_email_details: with your name, address, and apartment number.
rs_step_2: Look for large rent jumps in your apartment's history. If rent suddenly jumped above the deregulation threshold without a documented reason (e.g., major capital improvement), it may have been illegally deregulated.
rs_step_3: Check if preferential rent was used to inflate the legal registered rent above the threshold while charging you less.
rs_step_4_intro: File a complaint with DHCR if you suspect illegal deregulation. Under the Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act (2019), you may be entitled to rent overcharge refunds going back
